sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A school psychologist helped a teacher implement a token economy for a classroom in which students frequently interrupted instruction. Students earned tokens for raising their hands, waiting to be called on, and completing short independent tasks. Tokens could be exchanged for small privileges at the end of the week. Over several weeks, the teacher gradually shifted from continuous reinforcement to intermittent reinforcement. Interruptions declined, and students continued using the target behaviors even when tokens became less frequent.
questions.stem: Which behavior was the teacher trying to increase?

choices.A: Raising a hand, waiting to be called on, and completing tasks

explanations.A: Correct. Tokens were earned for hand-raising, waiting, and task completion. Interruptions declined, so the target behaviors were the appropriate classroom behaviors.
explanations.B: The passage does not support interrupting instruction more often; it points instead to raising a hand, waiting to be called on, and completing tasks.
explanations.C: The passage does not support leaving the classroom without permission; it points instead to raising a hand, waiting to be called on, and completing tasks.
explanations.D: The passage does not support ignoring independent work; it points instead to raising a hand, waiting to be called on, and completing tasks.

questions.stem: The token economy is based most directly on which process?

explanations.C: Correct. Tokens functioned as consequences delivered after target behaviors, which is the core structure of operant conditioning.
explanations.A: The passage does not support classical conditioning; it points instead to operant conditioning.
explanations.B: The passage does not support sensory adaptation; it points instead to operant conditioning.
explanations.D: The passage does not support object permanence; it points instead to operant conditioning.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.B: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.C: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.B: Correct. The passage describes a classroom intervention but does not isolate every possible contributor, such as teacher attention or classroom routines.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.C: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.D: Clearly define target behaviors and reinforce them consistently before thinning reinforcement
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.B: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.C: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.D: Correct. That application follows from using tokens to establish behavior and then shifting toward intermittent reinforcement for maintenance.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.B: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.C: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.B: Intermittent reinforcement can help maintain already learned behaviors after continuous reinforcement establishes them
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.C: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.B: Correct. The passage directly describes continued target behavior after tokens became less frequent.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.C: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.

questions.passage: A behavioral study examined smartphone checking during a 30-minute waiting-room task. Participants received notifications according to one of three schedules: fixed interval, fixed ratio, or variable ratio. The variable-ratio group checked the phone most often, averaging 22 checks. The fixed-ratio group averaged 16 checks, and the fixed-interval group averaged 11 checks. Participants in the variable-ratio group also reported the strongest urge to check when no notification had arrived for several minutes.
questions.stem: Which reinforcement schedule produced the most phone checking?

choices.D: All schedules produced the same checking rate

explanations.A: Correct. The variable-ratio group averaged 22 checks, more than the fixed-ratio group at 16 and the fixed-interval group at 11.
explanations.B: The passage does not support fixed interval; it points instead to variable ratio.
explanations.C: The passage does not support fixed ratio; it points instead to variable ratio.
explanations.D: The passage does not support all schedules produced the same checking rate; it points instead to variable ratio.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean number of phone checks across three notification reinforcement schedules.

questions.stem: Why can variable-ratio schedules produce persistent behavior?

choices.C: Rewards are unpredictable, so each response may be the one that pays off
choices.A: Rewards arrive at exact clock times
choices.B: The behavior is punished after every response
choices.D: The person receives no reinforcement at all

explanations.C: Correct. Variable-ratio reinforcement is powerful because reinforcement is uncertain but response-dependent, maintaining repeated checking.
explanations.A: The passage does not support rewards arrive at exact clock times; it points instead to rewards are unpredictable, so each response may be the one that pays off.
explanations.B: The passage does not support the behavior is punished after every response; it points instead to rewards are unpredictable, so each response may be the one that pays off.
explanations.D: The passage does not support the person receives no reinforcement at all; it points instead to rewards are unpredictable, so each response may be the one that pays off.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.D: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.B: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.C: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.D: Correct. The lab task supports a mechanism for checking behavior, but real-world smartphone habits may also involve social needs, work demands, and app design.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.B: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.C: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.A: Reduce unpredictable alerts and create planned checking times
choices.B: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.C: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.A: Correct. The variable-ratio pattern suggests unpredictability fuels checking, so reducing random cues should weaken the habit loop.
explanations.B: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.C: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.C: Notification schedules can influence checking behavior, especially when reinforcement is unpredictable
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.B: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.C: Correct. This conclusion matches the schedule differences without claiming all phone use is harmful.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.B: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.
